expectations.text: Recommends separating pure transformation logic from IO (S3 reads, DB writes)
expectations.evidence: Response says 'Keep transformation pure: no IO in the functions that reshape data' and shows read_records, validate_record, transform_record as separate functions.

expectations.text: Suggests splitting the monolithic DataProcessor into focused, single-responsibility pieces
expectations.evidence: Recommends 4 layers: Reader, Validation, Transformation, Writer. Each is a separate function/class.

expectations.text: Addresses how to compose the pieces together (pipeline, function composition, or similar)
expectations.evidence: run_pipeline() function shown that wires read_records -> validate_record -> transform_record -> write_records.

expectations.text: Includes at least one concrete code example illustrating the recommended structure
expectations.evidence: 5+ code blocks showing each layer with actual Python code.

expectations.text: Mentions explicit error handling at system boundaries (S3, DB)
expectations.evidence: db_connection context manager with rollback on exception; PipelineResult with records_skipped count; logger.warning for skipped records.

claims.claim: Old skill produced equally good output on this eval
claims.evidence: Both responses correctly recommend splitting into layers, keeping pure functions, and composing at the boundary. The old skill response is slightly less detailed (no Protocol-based abstractions, no test examples) but covers all 5 assertions.

suggestions.reason: Eval 1 may not discriminate well between old and new skill since both produce correct answers. Consider adding assertions about concrete code quality (e.g., 'includes Protocol-based abstractions for swappability', 'includes test examples showing how to test without infrastructure').

eval_feedback.overall: Both versions pass all assertions. The new skill response is richer (Protocol abstractions, test examples, before/after anti-pattern) but the assertions don't capture this difference.
